title: Check If Digits Are Equal in String After Operations II
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ performance proposals for this challenge. The minimum-footprint C Lucas/CRT variant is implemented in C; see crossReferences.

variants.languageReason: C++ gives native scalar arithmetic, inlined helper functions, and no GC/interpreter overhead; the streamed coefficient recurrence is O(n), allocation-free, and ideal for n <= 1e5.
variants.approachJustification: Compute the final equality as one binomial weighted difference modulo 10, maintaining each coefficient with separate 2/5 factor counts and an invertible unit residue.

variants.languageReason: C++ compact byte vectors and inlined loops make the factor precomputation cache-friendly; O(n) extra memory is acceptable for reducing repeated divisions in the coefficient loop.
variants.approachJustification: Precompute v2, v5, reduced unit residues, and inverse residues for all operands 1..n-2, then stream coefficients with array lookups.

reasoningSummary: The repeated adjacent-sum operation produces Pascal coefficients. Equality of the final digits is equivalent to one weighted adjacent-difference sum modulo 10. Because modulus 10 is composite, the C++ solutions stream coefficients by tracking powers of 2 and 5 separately from the invertible unit part.
timeComplexity: O(n) recommended and runtime / O(n log_5 n) memory
spaceComplexity: O(1) extra recommended / O(n) runtime / O(1) extra memory
